FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study cohort with baseline and 2-year follow-up structural imaging data; N=5,166 participants ages 9-10 at baseline

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using JIVE to identify brain co-development patterns, mediation analysis with baseline SMA predicting 2-year internalizing outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Baseline total SMA (self-reported hours/day on non-school SMAs) paired with internalizing psychopathology (CBCL) at 2-year follow-up; both individual-trait level. Direct effect β=0.020, P=0.014; mediation through brain co-development pattern (indirect effect=0.020, P=0.043)

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported: baseline SMA associated with internalizing at year 2 (β=0.020, SE=0.008, P=0.014) and with structural co-development pattern (β=0.015, SE=0.007, P=0.029)

FT_IC4.evidence: Mediation analysis with indirect effect quantified: indirect effect=0.020, p-value=0.043, proportion mediated=2.24%; direct effect of SMA on internalizing=0.82

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design but SM is both IV (baseline SMA predicting change) and DV-adjacent in mediation pathway; cross-sectional logic does not apply but EC7 carve-out ensures retention
notes: Main analysis reports baseline SMA → brain pattern → internalizing behavior pathway. Small effect sizes noted (2.24% mediated proportion). All criteria met for inclusion.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline sample; N=11,780 9-10 year-olds across 21 ABCD sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using K-SADS-5 clinical interviews and screen time self-report to examine associations with internalizing and externalizing disorder diagnoses

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Affective Disorders, 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Multiple phone/SM variables paired with individual-trait outcomes: total screen time, total digital media (social media, texting, gaming, online videos), TV/movies, video chat all analyzed against K-SADS internalizing disorder diagnoses (depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicidal ideation/attempts). Individual-trait level outcomes per DSM-5 criteria

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported in Tables 2-3: ORs for depressive disorder (2.06 for ≥2h/day vs. <2h/day for total screen time), anxiety disorders, self-harm, suicidal ideation/attempts across screen time categories and digital media types

FT_IC4.evidence: Extensive numeric results: unadjusted and adjusted ORs with 95% CI reported for all DV×exposure combinations; exposure-response curves examined; gender-stratified analyses provided

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design at baseline; EC7 does not apply to cross-sectional studies regardless of SM DV status
notes: Robust cross-sectional analysis with comprehensive screen time and digital media exposures paired with clinical DSM-5 diagnoses. Youth spending ≥2h/day with digital media (especially social media, texting, gaming, online videos) more likely to meet internalizing disorder criteria. All criteria met for inclusion.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; data from Release 3.0 with N~12,000 children ages 9-10 at baseline and Year 2 follow-up data

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a methods and psychometric validation paper. No empirical analysis of associations between SM/phone use and individual-trait outcomes. Paper reports: (1) screen usage questionnaire development and longitudinal changes in screen use, (2) psychometric properties and measurement invariance of SMAQ and VGAQ by sex and race/ethnicity, (3) demographic descriptives of screen use patterns. No analysis of external outcome correlates (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, personality, substance use, sleep, academic outcomes). Pure psychometric/validation paper per §2.4 binding clarification.

notes: FT-IC5 NOT_MET: Paper is a resource describing methods for screen usage assessment and psychometric analyses of SMAQ/VGAQ in ABCD. No new analysis linking SM/phone variables to health, behavioral, or other individual-trait outcomes. Explicitly methodological/descriptive in scope.
